card.indications: Management of mild to moderate pain, often in combination with a nonopioid analgesic, and suppression of cough in selected products. Use is limited by variable CYP2D6 metabolism and serious respiratory risks.
card.mechanism: A portion of codeine is converted by CYP2D6 to morphine, which activates mu-opioid receptors in the central nervous system. This reduces pain perception and cough reflex but also depresses respiration and GI motility.
card.dosing: Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Adult pain dosing is product specific and commonly given every 4 to 6 hours as needed. Combination products are limited by the maximum safe daily dose of acetaminophen or other ingredients. Renal, hepatic, and older patients need cautious dosing.
card.routeAdministration: May be taken with food to reduce nausea. Measure liquid with an accurate device. Do not crush extended-release combination products. Assess pain, sedation, respiratory rate, and cumulative acetaminophen exposure before repeat doses.
card.contraindications: Children younger than 12 years; postoperative pain after tonsillectomy or adenoidectomy in patients younger than 18; significant respiratory depression; acute or severe bronchial asthma without monitoring; known or suspected GI obstruction; and hypersensitivity. Breastfeeding is generally not recommended.
card.warnings: Boxed opioid warnings include addiction, abuse, misuse, life-threatening respiratory depression, accidental ingestion, neonatal opioid withdrawal, CYP2D6 ultra-rapid metabolism, and profound sedation or death with benzodiazepines or other CNS depressants.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, constipation, itching, dry mouth, and sweating.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Fatal respiratory depression, apnea, severe hypotension, ileus, adrenal insufficiency, seizures, serotonin syndrome with interacting drugs, anaphylaxis, addiction, overdose, and severe withdrawal.
card.interactions: Benzodiazepines, alcohol, sleep medications, gabapentinoids, antihistamines, and other CNS depressants increase respiratory depression. CYP2D6 inhibitors may reduce analgesia; CYP3A4 inhibitors or inducers can alter exposure. MAO inhibitors and serotonergic drugs can cause serious reactions.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain or cough response, respiratory rate, oxygenation, sedation score, blood pressure, bowel function, misuse risk, and total dose of combination ingredients. Naloxone access should be considered based on overdose risk.
card.patientTeaching: Take only as prescribed, avoid alcohol and sedatives, and do not drive. Store locked away and dispose of leftovers safely. Seek emergency help for slow or difficult breathing, blue lips, inability to awaken, or severe confusion. Use prescribed constipation prevention.
card.nutrition: Take with food if nauseated. Increase fluids and fiber unless restricted. Avoid alcohol completely. Check all OTC products for acetaminophen when using a combination medication.

card.indications: Treatment and prophylaxis of gout flares in adults and treatment of familial Mediterranean fever in appropriate patients. Some capsule products are labeled only for gout-flare prophylaxis, so indication and dose depend on the exact product.
card.mechanism: Binds to tubulin and inhibits microtubule polymerization, reducing neutrophil activation, migration, and inflammatory response to urate crystals. It does not lower serum uric acid and is not a general analgesic.
card.dosing: For an acute adult gout flare, a common tablet regimen is 1.2 mg at the first sign followed by 0.6 mg one hour later. Prophylaxis commonly uses 0.6 mg once or twice daily. Familial Mediterranean fever dosing is individualized by age and response. Reduce or avoid dosing in renal or hepatic impairment and with interacting drugs.
card.routeAdministration: May be taken with or without food. Take at the first sign of a gout flare only as prescribed and do not repeat a course sooner than directed. Measure liquid accurately. Product formulations and approved uses are not always interchangeable.
card.contraindications: Concurrent use of a strong CYP3A4 or P-glycoprotein inhibitor in a patient with renal or hepatic impairment. Avoid in severe renal or hepatic disease unless specialist-directed. Use caution with blood dyscrasias, neuromuscular disease, and older or frail patients.
card.warnings: Colchicine has a narrow therapeutic index. Overdose can be fatal. Early toxicity often begins with severe diarrhea, vomiting, or abdominal pain and can progress to marrow failure, neuromuscular toxicity, multiorgan failure, and death. Serious interactions can occur even at therapeutic doses.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramping, and abdominal pain.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Pancytopenia, agranulocytosis, aplastic anemia, rhabdomyolysis, neuromyopathy, severe hepatic or renal injury, disseminated intravascular coagulation, arrhythmias, multiorgan failure, and fatal overdose.
card.interactions: Strong CYP3A4 or P-glycoprotein inhibitors such as clarithromycin, certain azole antifungals, protease inhibitors, cyclosporine, and some calcium-channel blockers can cause fatal toxicity. Statins, fibrates, and digoxin may increase myopathy risk. Grapefruit can increase exposure.
card.monitoring: Monitor flare frequency, pain and swelling, CBC, renal and hepatic function, CK when muscle symptoms occur, GI tolerance, and all new medications. Assess for weakness, numbness, bruising, infection, or reduced urine output.
card.patientTeaching: Never exceed the prescribed dose and keep the drug away from children. Stop and call the prescriber for severe diarrhea or vomiting. Seek urgent care for muscle weakness, numbness, unusual bruising, fever, or reduced urine. Check before taking any antibiotic or antifungal.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Avoid grapefruit unless the prescriber confirms it is safe. Maintain hydration during a flare. Follow the broader gout plan, including moderation of alcohol and high-purine foods, but do not use diet changes as a substitute for prescribed therapy.
